title_en: Taharah: Points of Agreement and Difference
content_en:
Ibn Rushd opens Bidayat al-Mujtahid with the topic of taharah (ritual purification), as all classical legal manuals do. His treatment here sets the pattern for the rest of the book: he identifies what scholars agree on, what they differ on, and why the differences arose — tracing each dispute back to its source in the texts or in interpretive principles.

All four Sunni schools agree on the basic nature of purification: it is a precondition for prayer, and it consists of removing ritual impurity (hadath) either through wudu (minor purification) or ghusl (major purification). They agree that water is the primary agent of purification, that the face, hands, head, and feet are the areas specified in the Quran for wudu, and that the entire body must be washed in ghusl.

The disagreements begin at the level of detail. On the wiping of the head in wudu, the Quran says (5:6) to 'wipe your heads' without specifying how much. The Maliki school requires wiping the entire head, arguing that the Arabic particle 'ba' (in wamsahu bi-ru'usikum) does not indicate partiality. The Shafi'i and Hanbali schools hold that wiping any part of the head suffices, since the 'ba' indicates a partitive sense in Arabic. The Hanafi school holds that wiping a quarter of the head suffices, based on a specific narration. Ibn Rushd identifies the root of the disagreement as a difference in Arabic grammatical analysis of the particle 'ba' — a linguistic dispute with legal consequences.

On touching women as a nullifier of wudu, the Quran mentions 'touching women' among the conditions requiring purification (4:43, 5:6). The Shafi'i school takes this literally: any skin-to-skin contact between a marriageable man and woman nullifies wudu. The Maliki school requires that the contact be accompanied by pleasure or its intent. The Hanafi school interprets 'touching' as a euphemism for sexual intercourse, so skin contact alone does not nullify wudu in their view. The root of this disagreement is whether the Quranic term lams (touching) is to be understood literally or metaphorically — a classic example of the schools differing over the interpretation of an ambiguous Quranic expression.

For ghusl, the schools agree on its obligation after sexual intercourse, ejaculation, menstruation, and post-natal bleeding. They disagree on whether rinsing the mouth and nostrils is obligatory within ghusl (Shafi'i and Hanbali hold it obligatory; Maliki and Hanafi hold it recommended). They also disagree on whether a single immersion of the body in water with intention constitutes a valid ghusl, or whether deliberate washing of each part is required.

Tayammum — dry purification with clean earth — is agreed upon as permissible when water is absent or harmful. The schools differ on how many strikes of the earth are required (one or two) and whether tayammum is tied to a single prayer time or remains valid until a nullifier occurs. Ibn Rushd traces each difference to the varying interpretations of the Quranic verse on tayammum (5:6) and the relevant hadiths.

This comparative analysis of taharah demonstrates the book's central thesis: scholarly disagreement in fiqh is neither arbitrary nor irreconcilable — it is the product of consistent application of different but legitimate interpretive principles to shared sources.
content_id:
Ibn Rushd membuka Bidayat al-Mujtahid dengan topik taharah (bersuci secara ritual), sebagaimana semua manuskrip hukum klasik. Pembahasannya di sini menetapkan pola untuk sisa buku: beliau mengidentifikasi apa yang disepakati para ulama, apa yang mereka perselisihkan, dan mengapa perbedaan-perbedaan itu muncul — menelusuri setiap perselisihan kembali ke sumbernya dalam teks-teks atau prinsip-prinsip penafsiran.

Keempat mazhab Sunni sepakat tentang hakikat dasar taharah: ia adalah syarat sebelum salat, dan terdiri dari menghilangkan hadas baik melalui wudu (bersuci kecil) maupun mandi wajib (bersuci besar). Mereka sepakat bahwa air adalah agen utama taharah, bahwa wajah, tangan, kepala, dan kaki adalah anggota yang ditetapkan Al-Quran untuk wudu, dan bahwa seluruh tubuh harus dibasuh dalam mandi wajib.

Perbedaan-perbedaan mulai muncul pada tingkat perincian. Mengenai mengusap kepala dalam wudu, Al-Quran menyatakan (5:6) untuk 'mengusap kepala kalian' tanpa menyebutkan seberapa banyak. Mazhab Maliki mensyaratkan mengusap seluruh kepala, berargumen bahwa partikel Arab 'ba' (dalam wamsahu bi-ru'usikum) tidak menunjukkan kebagianan. Mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali berpendapat bahwa mengusap bagian mana pun dari kepala sudah mencukupi, karena 'ba' menunjukkan makna partitif dalam bahasa Arab. Mazhab Hanafi berpendapat bahwa mengusap seperempat kepala sudah mencukupi, berdasarkan riwayat tertentu. Ibn Rushd mengidentifikasi akar perbedaan ini sebagai perbedaan dalam analisis gramatikal Arab atas partikel 'ba' — perselisihan linguistik dengan konsekuensi hukum.

Mengenai menyentuh perempuan sebagai pembatal wudu, Al-Quran menyebutkan 'menyentuh perempuan' di antara kondisi-kondisi yang mensyaratkan taharah (4:43, 5:6). Mazhab Syafi'i mengambil ini secara harfiah: kontak kulit langsung antara laki-laki dan perempuan yang bukan mahram membatalkan wudu. Mazhab Maliki mensyaratkan bahwa sentuhan tersebut disertai syahwat atau niatnya. Mazhab Hanafi menafsirkan 'menyentuh' sebagai eufemisme untuk hubungan seksual, sehingga kontak kulit saja tidak membatalkan wudu menurut pandangan mereka. Akar perbedaan ini adalah apakah istilah Al-Quran lams (menyentuh) harus dipahami secara harfiah atau metaforis — contoh klasik mazhab-mazhab berbeda dalam menafsirkan ungkapan Al-Quran yang ambigu.

Untuk mandi wajib, mazhab-mazhab sepakat mengenai kewajibannya setelah hubungan seksual, ejakulasi, haid, dan nifas. Mereka berbeda pendapat apakah berkumur dan membersihkan hidung wajib dalam mandi wajib (mazhab Syafi'i dan Hanbali menganggapnya wajib; mazhab Maliki dan Hanafi menganggapnya sunnah). Mereka juga berbeda pendapat apakah menyelam sekali ke dalam air dengan niat merupakan mandi wajib yang sah, atau apakah diperlukan pembasuhan deliberat pada setiap anggota tubuh.

Tayamum — bersuci kering menggunakan tanah yang bersih — disepakati sebagai dibolehkan ketika air tidak ada atau berbahaya. Mazhab-mazhab berbeda mengenai berapa kali tepukan ke tanah yang diperlukan (satu atau dua) dan apakah tayamum terikat pada satu waktu salat atau tetap berlaku hingga pembatal terjadi. Ibn Rushd menelusuri setiap perbedaan ke penafsiran yang beragam atas ayat Al-Quran tentang tayamum (5:6) dan hadis-hadis yang relevan.

Analisis komparatif taharah ini mendemonstrasikan tesis sentral buku: ketidaksepakatan ilmiah dalam fikih tidaklah sewenang-wenang maupun tidak dapat didamaikan — ia adalah produk dari penerapan konsisten prinsip-prinsip penafsiran yang berbeda namun legitim terhadap sumber-sumber yang dianut bersama.
content_ar: Ibn Rushd opens Bidayat al-Mujtahid with the topic of الطهارة (ritual purification), as all الكلاسيكي legal manuals do. His treatment here sets the pattern for the rest of the الكتاب: he identifies what العلماء agree on, what they differ on, and why the differences arose — tracing each dispute back to its source in the texts or in interpretive principles. All four Sunni schools agree on the الأساسي nature of purification: it is a precondition for الصلاة, and it consists of removing ritual impurity (hadath) either through الوضوء (minor purification) or الغسل (major purification). They agree that water is the الأساسي agent of purification, that the face, hands, head, and feet are the areas specified in the القرآن الكريم for الوضوء, and that the entire الجسد must be washed in الغسل. The disagreements begin at the level of detail. On the wiping of the head in الوضوء, the القرآن الكريم says (5:6) to 'wipe your heads' without specifying how much. The المالكي school requires wiping the entire head, arguing that the Arabic particle 'ba' (in wamsahu bi-ru'usikum) does not indicate partiality. The الشافعي and الحنبلي schools hold that wiping any part of the head suffices, بما أن the 'ba' indicates a partitive sense in Arabic. The الحنفي school holds that wiping a quarter of the head suffices, based on a specific narration. Ibn Rushd identifies the root of the disagreement as a difference in Arabic grammatical analysis of the particle 'ba' — a linguistic dispute with legal consequences. On touching women as a nullifier of الوضوء, the القرآن الكريم mentions 'touching women' among the conditions requiring purification (4:43, 5:6). The الشافعي school takes this literally: any skin-to-skin contact between a marriageable man and woman nullifies الوضوء. The المالكي school requires that the contact be accompanied by pleasure or its intent. The الحنفي school interprets 'touching' as a euphemism for sexual intercourse, لذا skin contact alone does not nullify الوضوء in their view. The root of this disagreement is whether the القرآني term lams (touching) is to be understood literally or metaphorically — a classic example of the schools differing over the interpretation of an ambiguous القرآني expression. For الغسل, the schools agree on its الالتزام بعد sexual intercourse, ejaculation, menstruation, and post-natal bleeding. They disagree on whether rinsing the mouth and nostrils is obligatory within الغسل (الشافعي and الحنبلي hold it obligatory; المالكي and الحنفي hold it recommended). They أيضاً disagree on whether a single immersion of the الجسد in water with intention constitutes a valid الغسل, or whether deliberate washing of each part is required. التيمم — dry purification with clean earth — is agreed upon as permissible عندما water is absent or harmful. The schools differ on how many strikes of the earth are required (one or two) and whether التيمم is tied to a single الصلاة time or remains valid حتى a nullifier occurs.
